questions.held_out_passage: this made such an impression on me, that I could not get the idea the next day out of my mind, and I then became as anxious to see her as I was before indifferent; accordingly in the evening, after we left off working, I inquired where she lived, and being directed to her, to my inexpressible surprise, beheld the very woman in the very same dress she appeared to me to wear in the vision.

questions.text: In his work, how would he likely behave when he thought greater effort might shorten a difficult undertaking and improve his prospects?

questions.text: When brought near death by illness, what kind of inward response would he most likely have?
questions.held_out_passage: I was very ill for eleven days and near dying; eternity was now exceedingly impressed on my mind, and I feared very much that awful event. I prayed the Lord therefore to spare me; and I made a promise in my mind to God, that I would be good if ever I should recover.

questions.text: After recovering from danger and returning to ordinary business, how might his religious resolutions change over time?
questions.held_out_passage: During the passage, as I was perfectly restored, and had much business of the vessel to mind, all my endeavours to keep up my integrity, and perform my promise to God, began to fail; and, in spite of all I could do, as we drew nearer and nearer to the islands, my resolutions more and more declined, as if the very air of that country or climate seemed fatal to piety. When we were safe arrived at Montserrat, and I had got ashore, I forgot my former resolutions.

questions.text: If treated unfairly in a commercial exchange because others looked down on him, how would he likely feel and respond?
questions.held_out_passage: There was one gentleman particularly who bought a puncheon of rum of me, which gave me a great deal of trouble; and, although I used the interest of my friendly captain, I could not obtain any thing for it; for, being a negro man, I could not oblige him to pay me. This vexed me much, not knowing how to act; and I lost some time in seeking after this Christian;

questions.text: When trying to recover what was owed to him, would he likely persist even at personal inconvenience and at the cost of preferred activities?
questions.held_out_passage: and though, when the Sabbath came (which the negroes usually make their holiday) I was much inclined to go to public worship, I was obliged to hire some black men to help to pull a boat across the water to God in quest of this gentleman.

questions.text: If pressured into accepting an unjust or inferior payment because of his vulnerable position, what kind of treatment would he likely endure?
questions.held_out_passage: When I found him, after much entreaty, both from myself and my worthy captain, he at last paid me in dollars; some of them, however, were copper, and of consequence of no value; but he took advantage of my being a negro man, and obliged me to put up with those or none, although I objected to them.

questions.text: If suddenly threatened with violent punishment by hostile strangers without fair hearing, how would he most likely react to protect himself?
questions.held_out_passage: Immediately after, as I was trying to pass them in the market, amongst other white men, I was abused for offering to pass bad coin; and, though I shewed them the man I got them from, I was within one minute of being tied up and flogged without either judge or jury; however, by the help of a good pair of heels, I ran off, and so escaped the bastinadoes I should have received.

questions.text: When troubled by moral and spiritual anxiety, would he interpret vivid dreams or night experiences in religious terms?
questions.held_out_passage: In these severe conflicts the Lord answered me by awful 'visions of the night, when deep sleep falleth upon men, in slumberings upon the bed,' Job xxxiii. 15. He was pleased, in much mercy, to give me to see, and in some measure to understand, the great and awful scene of the judgment-day, that 'no unclean person, no unholy thing, can enter into the kingdom of God,' Eph. v. 5.

questions.text: If he felt he had sinned grievously, would he respond with repentance and direct prayer for mercy and amendment?

questions.text: After believing he had received divine mercy, would he make a specific personal vow concerning his speech and conduct?
questions.held_out_passage: This was the first spiritual mercy I ever was sensible of, and being on praying ground, as soon as I recovered a little strength, and got out of bed and dressed myself, I invoked Heaven from my inmost soul, and fervently begged that God would never again permit me to blaspheme his most holy name.

questions.text: Would he judge his abilities as something entrusted for a higher purpose rather than merely for personal advantage?
questions.held_out_passage: I felt that I was altogether unholy, and saw clearly what a bad use I had made of the faculties I was endowed with; they were given me to glorify God with; I thought, therefore, I had better want them here, and enter into life eternal, than abuse them and be cast into hell fire.

questions.text: If dissatisfied with the moral character of the people around him, would he actively seek out more devout companions and guidance?
questions.held_out_passage: I prayed to be directed, if there were any holier than those with whom I was acquainted, that the Lord would point them out to me.

questions.text: When living among people whose behavior offended his conscience, would he withdraw from that environment rather than remain comfortably in it?

'Before they call, I will answer; and while they are yet speaking, I will hear.' I had a great desire to read the bible the whole day at home; but not having a convenient place for retirement, I left the house in the day, rather than stay amongst the wicked ones;

questions.text: If he encountered an experienced and pious stranger who spoke deeply about religion, would he respond with eager questioning and receptiveness?
questions.held_out_passage: and that day as I was walking, it pleased God to direct me to a house where there was an old sea-faring man, who experienced much of the love of God shed abroad in his heart. He began to discourse with me; and, as I desired to love the Lord, his conversation rejoiced me greatly; and indeed I had never heard before the love of Christ to believers set forth in such a manner, and in so clear a point of view. Here I had more questions to put to the man than his time would permit him to answer;

questions.text: When asked about his religious practice by someone more doctrinally precise, would he answer plainly from his own understanding even if he did not fully grasp the terms being used?

I knew not what he meant by hearing the gospel; I told him I had read the gospel: and he asked where I went to church, or whether I went at all or not. To which I replied, 'I attended St. James's, St. Martin's, and St. Ann's, Soho;'--'So,' said he, 'you are a churchman.' I answered, I was.
